pre_sync_row.title: ISigmaSeparation
pre_sync_row.addition_to_the_LN: [sigma_symmetry_claim_invokes_unstated_reversal_invariance] In a CDMG, a walk is a sequence of incident edges without regard to edge orientation; consequently, every walk from $A$ to $B$ is, read in reverse, a walk from $B$ to $A$, and this reversal is an involution on the set of walks. The symmetry of $\sigma$-separation (when $J = \emptyset$) is to be understood as resting on this walk-reversal involution together with the fact that the $\sigma$-blocking conditions on internal nodes are stated in a manner invariant under reversal.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: empty_b_non_vacuous_when_j_nonempty
explanation: The literal definition of $A \isPerp_G B \given C$ requires "every walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$" to be $\sigma$-blocked. Consider the corner case $B = \emptyset$ with $J \neq \emptyset$. One might naively expect "separation from the empty set" to be vacuously true (standard separoid intuition: $A \perp \emptyset \given C$ holds trivially because there's nothing to separate from). But here $J \cup B = J \cup \emptyset = J$ is nonempty, so $A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$ literally asserts that every walk from $A$ into the input nodes $J$ is $\sigma$-blocked â€” a non-trivial condition, not a vacuous truth. This is a direct consequence of the (sic!) choice to include $J$ in the target, but the footnote justifies it only via separoid-rules-matching-Markov-kernels, not by explicitly flagging this loss of the usual vacuity. A reader who sees "$A \isPerp_G \emptyset \given C$" downstream and reaches for vacuity would be wrong whenever $J \neq \emptyset$.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: symmetry_claim_walks_between_wording_imprecise
explanation: The embedded claim (marked `% claim_3_22`) justifies $\sigma$-separation symmetry by saying "when $J = \emptyset$ the set of walks between $A$ and $B$ is the same regardless of direction". Read literally, this is imprecise: walks in a CDMG are directed sequences of vertices and edges, so the *set* of walks from $A$ to $B$ is not literally equal to the *set* of walks from $B$ to $A$. What is true is that walk *reversal* induces a bijection between these two sets, and the $\sigma$-blocking property is invariant under that bijection (because the collider/non-collider role of each internal vertex is preserved by reversal). The current wording conflates "set equality" with "bijection-preserving-blocking-status", which weakens the proof sketch: a careful reader trying to formalize this claim from the literal text would have to recover the missing reversal-bijection step themselves. This matters if claim_3_22 is later proved from this justification.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: overlap_with_j_or_target_creates_self_walks
explanation: The definition explicitly permits $A, B, C \ins J \cup V$ "not necessarily disjoint". Combined with the asymmetric inclusion of $J$ on the right ("walks from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$"), this creates a literal-reading corner case: if $A \cap (J \cup B) \neq \emptyset$ â€” in particular if $A \cap J \neq \emptyset$, or if $A \cap B \neq \emptyset$, or even if $A = B$ â€” then every node $a \in A \cap (J \cup B)$ is simultaneously a starting node *and* an endpoint of the "walk from a node in $A$ to a node in $J \cup B$" quantification. Whether this forces trivial/length-zero walks (e.g. the walk $\langle a \rangle$) to be required to be $\sigma$-blocked depends entirely on whether the upstream walk definition admits length-zero walks (a question this block leaves implicit). If it does, then e.g. $A \isPerp_G A \given C$ degenerates to a condition about every $a \in A$ having its trivial self-walk $\sigma$-blocked by $C$, which is a very different statement than the usual "separation of $A$ from itself". The tex block does not pin this down; downstream lemmas like "$A \isPerp_G B$ when $A = B \subseteq C$" could hinge on this convention.
END_SUBTLETY
